LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS C.R. BARD, INC. AND BARD PERIPHERAL VASCULAR, INC.'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS ON PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT. THE MATTER WAS ARGUED. THE COURT CONFIRMED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. GRANTED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE FOURTH AND FIFTH CAUSES OF ACTION FOR STRICT LIABILITY BASED ON DESIGN DEFECT, GRANTED WITH 20 DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO THE SEVENTH CAUSE OF ACTION FOR NEGLIGENT MISREPRESENTATION, AND DENIED AS TO THE SIXTH AND NINTH CAUSES OF ACTION FOR STRICT LIABILITY BASED ON MANUFACTURING DEFECT AND NEGLIGENCE BASED ON FAILURE TO RECALL THE PRODUCT. PLAINTIFF WAYNE RUDEN'S FOURTH AND FIFTH CAUSES OF ACTION FAIL AS A MATTER OF LAW BECAUSE A MANUFACTURER OF MEDICAL DEVICES CANNOT BE HELD STRICTLY LIABLE FOR DESIGN DEFECT BASED ON THE CONSUMER EXPECTATION AND RISK-BENEFIT TESTS. (BROWN V. SUPERIOR COURT (1988) 44 CAL.3D 1049, 1061-65; HUFT V. HOROWITZ (1992) 4 CAL.APP.4TH 8, 19-20.) AS TO THE SEVENTH CAUSE OF ACTION, MR. RUDEN DID NOT ALLEGE NEGLIGENT MISREPRESENTATION WITH SUFFICIENT SPECIFICITY. MR. RUDEN DOES NOT ALLEGE WHO MADE THE ALLEGED MISREPRESENTATIONS, TO WHOM THEY WERE MADE, OR WHEN THEY WERE MADE. THE SIXTH CAUSE OF ACTION IS ADEQUATELY PLED. MR. RUDEN ALLEGED BOTH CAUSATION AND THAT THE IMPLANTED MEDICAL DEVICE DIFFERS FROM THE MANUFACTURER'S INTENDED RESULT. (FAC, PARS.100, 103.) THE NINTH CAUSE OF ACTION IS ALSO ADEQUATELY PLED. RAMIREZ V. PLOUGH, INC. (1993) 6 CAL.4TH 539 DOES NOT DISCLAIM A COMMON LAW DUTY TO RECALL A MEDICAL DEVICE ABSENT AN FDA ORDER. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. REPORTER: SHERRY SAWYER, CSR #5976.

Off Calendar Feb-06-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearance.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF WAYNE RUDEN'S [Second] Motion To Compel Deposition And Deposition Answers Of CPMC's Person Most Qualified And For Sanctions, as follows:

Plaintiff Wayne Ruden's second motion to compel deposition responses from CPMC's person most qualified (Theresa Carney) and for monetary sanctions is granted in large part. Unless the parties agree otherwise defendant Sutter West Bay Hospitals (CPMC) must make Ms. Carney available for another session of her PMQ deposition on a mutually convenient date no later than February 28, 2018. At that deposition Ms. Carney must provide responses to all questions she was instructed not to answer at the PMQ deposition on August 22, 2017 including all reasonable follow-up questions that are consistent with the order filed December 18, 2017 except the two questions (24 and 25 on the separate statements) that begin "Hypothetically." Ms. Carney is not required to answer any questions based on an assumed or hypothetical set of facts. The determination of whether a question seeks information about physician credentialing and/or physician obtaining patient informed consent is not always an easy one and thus the court is not precluding Mr. Ruden from asking Ms. Carney what may appear to be credentialing questions of Ms. Carney if he has a good faith argument that the questions seek information about physician obtaining informed consent. For the reasons explained in the December 18, 2017 order, CPMC's instructions not to answer at the August 22, 2017 deposition lacked substantial justification. CPMC must pay Mr. Ruden sanctions in the amount of $2,814.85 no later than February 28, 2018, calculated as 7.8 hours of Mr. Turner's time at $300 per hour for the time he spent preparing for, taking and conferring with CPMC counsel about the August 22, 2017 deposition and costs of $474.85. No sanctions are awarded for the time spent by Mr. Ruden's counsel regarding this motion on or after October 18, 2017, the date that Mr. Diemer reasonably and with substantial justification proposed that the disputes regarding the August 22, 2017 deposition be deferred until after the then pending motion concerning the June 26, 2017 deposition were ruled on by this court.

Counsel for Mr. Ruden is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the Court's ruling and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: torreanom@aol.com; mtorreano@sftc.org.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant Sutter West Bay Hospitals' motion to strike punitive damages allegations in the second amended complaint is granted. Plaintiff Wayne Ruden acknowledges that he failed to comply with CCP 425.13 which requires that he obtain a court order before filing a pleading that requests punitive damages. Section 425.13 also requires that a party seeking punitive damages must submit evidence sufficient to establish a prima facie case for punitive damages. (Little Co. of Mary Hosp. v. Superior Court (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 261, 267). Thus, even if the court overlooked the failure of Mr. Ruden to obtain a court order prior to his seeking punitive damages, this motion must still be granted because Mr. Ruden has not submitted any evidence as required by section 425.13. AMENDED DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-13-2017 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT SUTTER WEST BAY HOSPITALS AMENDED DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT is a duplicate entry.

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-13-2017 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. PATRICIA S. LAKNER OF DAVIDOVITZ & BENNETT LAW, TEL. NO. 1-415-956-4800 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT;
NO OTHER APPEARANCES ON THE MATTER.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant Sutter West Bay Hospitals' demurrer to the second amended complaint is: a) overruled as to the second cause of action for negligence, b) sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the seventh cause of action for negligence misrepresentation, and c) sustained without leave to amend as to the eighth cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty and the ninth cause of action for negligence-recall/retrofit. As to the second cause of action, Plaintiff Wayne Ruden adequately alleges a negligence claim against CPMC. Mr. Ruden alleges a recognized theory of secondary liability and therefore a recognized duty to obtain informed consent. (Moore v. Regents of the Univ. of Cal. (1990) 51 Cal.3d 120, 133.) He also adequately alleges a legal duty on part of CPMC under the doctrine of corporate hospital negligence to ensure the competence of its physicians through careful selection and review. (Elam v. College Park Hospital (1982) 132 Cal.App.3d 332, 346.) Mr. Ruden did not adequately allege a negligent misrepresentation claim against CPMC. He has not alleged that CPMC made affirmative representations to him that the medical device was safe to be implanted or that CPMC intended that Mr. Ruden would rely on any of such affirmative representations. If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Ruden is granted leave to amend to cure these deficiencies. Mr. Ruden has not alleged a cognizable breach of fiduciary duty claim against CPMC since there is no duty imposed on a hospital to warn its patients of newly discovered product defects after conclusion of a medical procedure. The only possible cognizable claim for breach of fiduciary duty would be based on CPMC's alleged failure to obtain informed consent prior to implantation, but such a claim is entirely duplicative of the second cause of action for negligence. Mr. Ruden also fails to allege a cognizable claim for negligence based on a failure to recall the medical device against CPMC. Mr. Ruden's lack of opposition to the demurrer to the ninth cause of action is a concession that the demurrer to that claim is meritorious. There is no reasonable possibility that the deficiencies in the eighth and ninth causes of action against CPMC can be cured by amendment. Order signed. Bard, Inc. and Bard Peripheral Vascular, Inc.'s motion to strike punitive damages allegations in plaintiff's frist amended complaint is denied. Mr. Ruden adequately alleges entitlement to punitive damages. Mr. Ruden alleges that the moving defendants obtained regulatory approval for the device through deceptive means, knowing its dangerous propensities and unacceptable failure rate (FAC, par. 39); published and continue to publish data showing lower failure rates (FAC, par. 40); possessed actual knowledge of the dangers to the life, health, and well-being of plaintiff but consciously failed to act reasonably (FAC, par. 44); embarked on an aggressive campaign of "off-label marketing" where representations were made to physicians that the product was safe and effective for use before FDA approval (FAC, par. 43); and retained a regulatory specialist to assist in obtaining FDA approval after previous attempts failed since the device was defective (FAC, par. 45b). Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings On Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Oct-12-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Glenn Turner of Ongaro PC telephone number 415-433-3900 for Plaintiff Wayne Ruden.
Steven Boranian of Reed Smith LLP telephone number 415-659-5980 for Defendants C.R. Bard, Inc. and Bard Peripheral Vascular, Inc.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling. Defendants C.R. Bard, Inc. and Bard Peripheral Vascular, Inc.'s motion for judgment on the pleadings on plaintiff's first amended complaint is granted without leave to amend as to the fourth and fifth causes of action for strict liability based on design defect, granted with 20 days leave to amend as to the seventh cause of action for negligent misrepresentation, and denied as to the sixth and ninth causes of action for strict liability based on manufacturing defect and negligence based on failure to recall the product. Plaintiff Wayne Ruden's fourth and fifth causes of action fail as a matter of law because a manufacturer of medical devices cannot be held strictly liable for design defect based on the consumer expectation and risk-benefit tests. (Brown v. Superior Court (1988) 44 Cal.3d 1049, 1061-65; Huft v. Horowitz (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 8, 19-20.) As to the seventh cause of action, Mr. Ruden did not allege negligent misrepresentation with sufficient specificity. Mr. Ruden does not allege who made the alleged misrepresentations, to whom they were made, or when they were made. The sixth cause of action is adequately pled. Mr. Ruden alleged both causation and that the implanted medical device differs from the manufacturer's intended result. (FAC, pars.100, 103.) The ninth cause of action is also adequately pled. Ramirez v. Plough, Inc. (1993) 6 Cal.4th 539 does not disclaim a common law duty to recall a medical device absent an FDA order. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
